exercises.prompt: TWO SUM — the most-asked interview question on earth. Given an array and a target, return the INDICES [i, j] (i < j) of the two numbers that add to target. Exactly one solution exists. O(n) with a Map — no nested loops.
GROUP ANAGRAMS. Given an array of strings, group the anagrams together and return the groups (any order). Key insight: anagrams share a canonical form — the sorted word. Map<sortedWord, group>.

TOP K FREQUENT ELEMENTS. Return the k most frequent values in the array (any order). Count with a Map, then pick the top k. (A full sort of the entries is acceptable here; the heap upgrade comes later in the ladder.)

SUBARRAY SUM EQUALS K (the prefix-sum + hashmap classic). Count how many CONTIGUOUS subarrays sum to exactly k. Insight: if prefix[j] - prefix[i] = k, the slice (i..j] sums to k — so at each step ask the map how many times (runningSum - k) has appeared.
VALID PALINDROME. Ignoring non-alphanumeric characters and case, is the string a palindrome? Two pointers from both ends; skip junk characters inward.

TWO SUM ON A SORTED ARRAY. Same goal as Two Sum, but the array is sorted — so NO hashmap: two pointers, O(1) space. Return the 0-based indices [l, r]. Narrate (to yourself) why moving the pointer is safe.

CONTAINER WITH MOST WATER. heights[i] are wall heights; pick two walls holding the most water: area = min(h[l], h[r]) × (r - l). Greedy two pointers: always move the SHORTER wall inward — moving the taller one can never help (the min wouldn't grow, the width shrinks).

3SUM — the pattern's boss fight. Return all UNIQUE triplets summing to 0 (each triplet sorted ascending). Sort the array, fix one number, then run two pointers on the rest. Two dedup guards: skip repeated anchors, and skip repeated left values after a hit.
MAX SUM OF K CONSECUTIVE (fixed window warm-up). Return the maximum sum of any k consecutive elements. Do NOT re-sum each window — add the entering element, subtract the leaving one.

LONGEST SUBSTRING WITHOUT REPEATING CHARACTERS — the most famous sliding window. Variable window: expand right; on a repeat, evict from the left until clean. Track chars with a Set (or last-index Map for the jump optimization).

MINIMUM SIZE SUBARRAY SUM. Smallest length of a contiguous subarray with sum ≥ target (0 if none). Positive numbers only — which is exactly why shrink-while-valid works: expand right, then shrink left as long as the window still satisfies.

LONGEST REPEATING CHARACTER REPLACEMENT (the separator question). Longest substring that becomes all-one-letter after at most k replacements. Window is valid while (length − count of the most frequent char) ≤ k. Track letter counts; maxFreq may be kept lazily (it never needs to shrink — think about why the answer stays correct).
VALID PARENTHESES. Given a string of ()[]{}, is every bracket opened and closed in the right order? Push opens; on a close, the top of the stack must be its partner. End: stack empty.
EVALUATE REVERSE POLISH NOTATION. tokens like ['2','1','+','3','*'] → ((2+1)*3) = 9. Numbers push; operators pop TWO (mind the order for - and /), compute, push back. Division truncates toward zero.

DAILY TEMPERATURES (monotonic stack). For each day, how many days until a WARMER temperature? (0 if never.) Keep a stack of indices with decreasing temps; a warm arrival resolves everything colder on top.

BUILD A MIN-STACK (design question — real hands-on). A stack supporting push, pop, top, AND getMin, all O(1). Trick: a second stack tracking the minimum at each depth (push min(x, currentMin) alongside).
CLASSIC BINARY SEARCH. Sorted array, return the index of target or -1. Write it from muscle memory: lo <= hi, mid = (lo+hi)>>1, three branches. This must take you under 3 minutes — it's the alphabet.

LEFTMOST TRUE (lower bound) — the boundary skeleton. Given a sorted array, return the index of the FIRST element >= target (array length if none). This is the lo < hi converging form — the shape behind firstBadVersion, insert position, and every search-the-answer problem.

SEARCH IN ROTATED SORTED ARRAY. [4,5,6,7,0,1,2] is sorted-then-rotated; find target in O(log n). At every mid, ONE half is guaranteed sorted — check which, then test if target lies inside that sorted half to pick a side.

KOKO EATING BANANAS (search the ANSWER). piles of bananas, h hours; find the minimum eating speed k (bananas/hour) to finish in time — each hour she eats from one pile, ceil(pile/k) hours per pile. The answer space 1..max(piles) is monotonic: binary search it with a works(k) predicate.

REVERSE A LINKED LIST — the rite of passage. Nodes are { val, next }. Reverse in place with prev/curr/next; return the new head. The tests build chains for you and decode your result back to an array.

MIDDLE OF THE LINKED LIST (fast & slow runners). Return the middle node's VALUE; for even lengths return the second middle. Fast moves 2, slow moves 1 — when fast falls off, slow stands in the middle.

MERGE TWO SORTED LISTS (the zipper). Splice two ascending { val, next } lists into one sorted list. Use a dummy head so you never special-case the first node — say 'dummy node' out loud in interviews; it's a fluency signal.

LINKED LIST CYCLE (Floyd's runners). Return true if the list loops. Fast gains exactly one node per step on slow inside a cycle — they MUST meet. The tests hand-build a cycle; your job is just the two runners.

MAXIMUM DEPTH OF A BINARY TREE. Nodes are { val, left, right } (null = empty). The purest recursion: depth = 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)). Three lines. Feel the shape — every tree problem is a remix of it.
SAME TREE. Are two trees structurally identical with equal values? Recursion over PAIRS of nodes: both null → true; one null or values differ → false; else recurse on both sides.

VALIDATE BINARY SEARCH TREE — the famous trap question. Every node must be within an inherited (lo, hi) RANGE, not just bigger/smaller than its parent. The tree [5, 4, 6, null, null, 3, 7] defeats the naive parent-check: 3 < 6 locally, but 3 < 5 globally. Pass the range down.

LOWEST COMMON ANCESTOR IN A BST. Return the VALUE of the lowest node that has both p and q in its subtree (a node counts as its own ancestor). Use the BST property to steer: both smaller → go left; both bigger → go right; otherwise you're standing on the split point — the answer.

BINARY TREE LEVEL ORDER TRAVERSAL — the BFS skeleton itself. Return values grouped by level: [[3],[9,20],[15,7]]. The one critical line: freeze the queue length BEFORE the inner loop, or levels bleed together.
RIGHT SIDE VIEW. Standing to the right of the tree, which values do you see top-to-bottom? It's level order keeping only each level's LAST element — a one-line twist on the skeleton you just wrote.

MINIMUM DEPTH (why BFS beats DFS for 'nearest'). Shortest path from root to the FIRST leaf (a node with no children). BFS returns the moment it meets a leaf — it never explores deeper than the answer. Careful: a node with one child is NOT a leaf (the [2 → 3 → 4...] chain trap).

NUMBER OF ISLANDS — THE grid question. grid of '1' (land) / '0' (water); count connected islands (4-directional). Scan every cell; on unvisited land, count it and flood-fill (sink) the whole island so it's never counted again. The grid may be mutated.

ROTTING ORANGES (multi-source BFS). Grid: 0 empty, 1 fresh, 2 rotten. Every minute, rot spreads to adjacent fresh oranges. Return minutes until none fresh (-1 if unreachable). Seed the queue with ALL rotten oranges at minute 0 — the wave spreads from every source at once; minutes = BFS levels.

COURSE SCHEDULE (topological sort / cycle detection). numCourses and prerequisites [[a,b]] meaning b must come before a. Can you finish them all? Kahn's algorithm: compute indegrees, repeatedly take zero-indegree courses; if you process fewer than numCourses, a cycle blocks you.

BUILD A MIN-HEAP — from memory, no peeking at the card. Array-backed: parent (i-1)>>1, children 2i+1 / 2i+2. push = append + bubble up; pop = swap root with last + sift down. This class is your weapon for every top-k question that follows.

KTH LARGEST ELEMENT. Find the kth largest WITHOUT fully sorting: min-heap capped at size k — push each number, pop when size exceeds k. Survivors are the k largest; the root is the kth. O(n log k). (A MinHeap is provided in the starter.)

K CLOSEST POINTS TO ORIGIN. points [[x,y],...] — return the k closest to (0,0) (any order; compare squared distance, no sqrt needed). Same capped-heap idea — but now you need a MAX-heap of size k (farthest falls out). Trick: reuse a min-heap by pushing NEGATED distances, or write the comparator inline.

SUBSETS (the backtracking skeleton). Return ALL subsets of an array of unique numbers. Every node in the decision tree IS an answer — record a COPY of the path on entry, then branch with start=i+1. Feel the push → recurse → pop rhythm; it's the whole pattern.
PERMUTATIONS. All orderings of distinct numbers. The twist vs subsets: loop from 0 every time, with a used[] array so each element appears once per path. Record only FULL-LENGTH paths.

COMBINATION SUM. Unique candidates, unlimited reuse; return all unique combinations summing to target. The reuse twist: recurse with i (NOT i+1) so a number can pick itself again. Prune: sort first, break when the candidate exceeds the remaining target.

CLIMBING STAIRS — DP's 'hello world'. n steps, hop 1 or 2 at a time; how many distinct ways up? State: dp[i] = ways to stand on step i. Transition: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] (the last hop was 1 or 2). Roll it to two variables: O(1) space.

HOUSE ROBBER. Max loot from a row of houses where adjacent houses can't both be robbed. State: best up to house i. The one-line decision at every house: skip me (dp[i-1]) or take me plus best-two-ago (dp[i-2] + nums[i]).

COIN CHANGE — the canonical 'min cost' DP. Fewest coins to make amount (-1 if impossible). dp[a] = fewest coins for amount a; for each coin c ≤ a: dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-c] + 1). Base dp[0]=0, everything else starts at Infinity.

LONGEST INCREASING SUBSEQUENCE (O(n²) version — own it before any binary-search upgrade). dp[i] = length of the longest increasing subsequence ENDING at i: look back at every j < i with nums[j] < nums[i]. Answer = max over all endings.
UNIQUE PATHS (grid DP warm-up). Robot at top-left of an m×n grid, moves only right/down; how many paths to bottom-right? dp[r][c] = dp[r-1][c] + dp[r][c-1]; first row and column are all 1.

LONGEST COMMON SUBSEQUENCE — the canonical two-string table. dp[i][j] = LCS length of a's first i chars vs b's first j. Match → diagonal + 1; else max of dropping one char from either side. Row/col 0 = 0 (empty prefix).

EDIT DISTANCE — FAANG's favorite hard-but-fair. Minimum operations (insert/delete/replace one char) to turn word1 into word2. Same table as LCS but the bases matter: dp[i][0] = i (delete everything), dp[0][j] = j (insert everything). Match → diagonal; else 1 + min(replace, delete, insert).
MERGE INTERVALS. Merge all overlapping [start, end] pairs. Sort by START, then sweep once: overlap (s <= lastEnd) extends the current interval; otherwise start a new one. Don't mutate the input.

NON-OVERLAPPING INTERVALS (the end-sort greedy). Minimum number of intervals to REMOVE so none overlap (touching endpoints don't overlap). Sort by END and greedily keep what fits — earliest-end leaves maximum room. Answer = total − kept. Be ready to defend WHY end-sort is optimal.

JUMP GAME (greedy frontier). nums[i] = max jump length from i; can you reach the last index? One pass tracking the furthest reachable index — if you ever stand past the frontier, you've found an uncrossable gap.
